result:
# The Cartography of Forgetting

I've been thinking about maps lately. Not Google Maps, not the blue pulsing dot that tells you where you are with such confidence that it has nearly eliminated the particular anxiety of being lost—but old maps, the kind drawn by people who were working at the edge of what anyone knew, who had to decide what to do with the blank spaces.

Medieval cartographers sometimes filled those spaces with monsters. Sea serpents coiling around the margins of the known world. Elephants in Africa, not entirely inaccurate but drawn so strangely that they looked like creatures from fever dreams. The phrase *hic sunt dracones*—here be dragons—appears on only one surviving map from that period, the Lenox Globe, cast in copper sometime around 1510. We've turned it into a cultural shorthand for the terrifying unknown, but it appeared exactly once, and we don't entirely know what the engraver meant by it. Maybe he was being precise. Maybe he'd talked to sailors. Maybe he was bored.

What I find more interesting than the monsters is the alternative approach: some cartographers simply left the spaces blank. White vellum, nothing. A kind of cartographic honesty that said: we know there is land here because the ocean has an edge, but we cannot tell you what that land holds. Here is the shape of our ignorance.

I think about that choice—monsters versus blankness—as a genuine philosophical fork. It applies to almost every domain of human thinking.

---

There is a woman I knew in college who had an extraordinary memory. Not photographic, nothing so clinical as that, but she remembered the emotional texture of things with unusual fidelity. She could tell you exactly how she had felt at fourteen watching a specific film, what the air in the theater was like, what she'd been worried about that week, how those worries had colored the film and how the film had colored the worries. She was a vivid, generous person, and her memories seemed like gifts she could give you—she could describe a shared experience and make you feel you'd been there, even if you hadn't.

But she was also, by her own admission, miserable in a low-grade persistent way that she couldn't entirely explain. She saw a therapist for years. The therapist, she told me once, had said something that stuck with her: "You have an extraordinary ability to remember how you felt. I wonder if you have an equally extraordinary ability to keep feeling it."

This has stayed with me for years. I keep turning it over.

There's a theory in cognitive neuroscience—simplified here, necessarily—that memory is not retrieval but reconstruction. Every time you remember something, you are not accessing a file; you are rebuilding it from pieces, and the act of rebuilding changes the pieces slightly. Your memories are plastic, living things. This is why eyewitness testimony is famously unreliable. This is why siblings can have completely different memories of the same childhood. You are not a camera. You are something stranger and more creative than a camera.

The implication, which I find either comforting or disturbing depending on my mood, is that forgetting is not a failure. It might be a feature. The rough edges of experience wear smooth. The sharpness of an old embarrassment dulls. You integrate things, metabolize them, and what you're left with is a kind of sediment—not the original event but something the event became through years of being remembered and revised.

My friend with the extraordinary memory had, perhaps, a metabolism that ran differently. She couldn't let things become sediment. She kept re-experiencing them with something like their original intensity.

I don't know if she was better or worse off than the rest of us. I genuinely don't know. There's an argument that she was living more honestly—that the rest of us were merely in a comfortable fog, that our sense of having "moved on" was really just the brain's merciful editing department doing its work without our consent. And there's an argument that what the brain does without our consent is part of what we are, that the forgetting is us just as much as the remembering is.

---

I want to talk about something only tangentially related, which is the way certain kinds of knowledge feel in the body before they reach the mind.

I learned to drive when I was seventeen, in a beige sedan that belonged to my mother, in the empty parking lot of a church that sat quiet all week except on Sundays. The learning was awkward and frightening in the way that new physical skills often are—not because the body can't do it but because the mind keeps interfering, keeps trying to narrate and manage a process that needs to become, eventually, unconscious. You cannot drive well while thinking about driving. The thinking is a scaffold, and at some point the scaffold has to come down.

When I drive now, I don't know how I drive. I can't explain how I know how far the car extends to my left, how I modulate pressure on the gas when the light ahead is turning yellow, how I read the behavior of other drivers from subtle cues that I couldn't name. The knowledge is in my hands and feet and the way my eyes move. Ask me to describe it and I'll fail. But give me a car and I'll drive it.

This is what Michael Polanyi called tacit knowledge—the things we know but cannot tell. He thought it was actually more fundamental than the explicit knowledge we can articulate, that under all our careful propositions there was this vast dark continent of embodied understanding that we navigated without maps. *We know more than we can tell*, is his famous line. Simple, and once you notice it, everywhere.

What's strange about tacit knowledge is how it challenges the usual relationship between understanding and articulation. We tend to think that if you really understand something, you can explain it. That explanation is the proof of understanding. But the driver who's been driving for thirty years understands driving in a way that no seventeen-year-old reading a manual does, even if the seventeen-year-old can articulate the rules more precisely. The sommelier who can identify a grape variety by smell understands something the textbook writer might not. The carpenter who knows by the sound of a knock whether a wall is load-bearing knows something structural engineers know differently and perhaps less usefully.

Our education systems are built almost entirely around the explicit, the articulable. We test what can be written down. Which means we are measuring, always, the scaffolding—and often the scaffolding of people who've had to put the scaffolding back up to show us what they know, the actual building temporarily hidden.

---

Here is something I believe, which I can't fully defend: that the most important things tend to arrive obliquely.

You cannot decide to fall in love, though you can create conditions where it becomes possible. You cannot decide to be inspired, though you can sit at the desk and do the work and leave the door open. You cannot think your way to the conviction that your life is worth living—the conviction has to come from somewhere else, from the accumulated texture of days, from what you notice when you're not watching yourself.

This is why I'm suspicious of the productivity literature. Not the practical stuff—systems for managing tasks, techniques for concentration—but the deeper promise, the one that says the examined life is the optimized life, that if you track enough variables about yourself you'll be able to engineer the version of you that you want. There's something in me that resists this. It seems like a category error. It's treating yourself as a machine you're also the operator of, when the more honest admission is that you are a machine that sometimes gets to observe itself and occasionally influence its own behavior at the margins.

I'm not making a mystical argument. I'm not saying there's a soul that stands outside the mechanism. I'm saying the mechanism is vastly more complex than the part of it doing the observing, and that the observing part should probably maintain a certain humility about its ability to run the show.

---

Back to the maps.

In 1507, a German cartographer named Martin Waldseemüller produced a map of the world—the first to name the new continents "America," a name that stuck despite Waldseemüller's later doubts about whether Amerigo Vespucci deserved the honor. The map is enormous, twelve panels, four feet by eight feet when assembled. It sold thousands of copies and then disappeared almost entirely. For centuries, no copy was known to survive. Then in 1901, a Jesuit historian found a single surviving copy folded in a bookcase in a German castle.

There's one thing about the map that I find almost unbearable in its poignancy. Waldseemüller drew a South America with a recognizable Pacific coast—a coast that no European had officially seen yet. Magellan's circumnavigation was still more than a decade away. How did he know to draw it?

Historians have various explanations. Portuguese maps, kept secret. Unofficial voyages never recorded in official histories. Something. The knowledge existed somewhere, imperfectly, and it made its way into the cartographer's hand.

I think about the cartographer sitting with information that didn't fit any authorized narrative of what was known. He had to decide what to do with it. He drew it in.

There's courage in that, or at least a kind of commitment to the thing in front of you over the story you're supposed to be telling. He didn't know why he had information he wasn't supposed to have. He just had it, and the map needed a western coast of South America, and he drew one.

---

I keep returning to a particular quality of attention that seems related to everything I've been circling here. I want to try to describe it directly, though I suspect it will escape me.

It is the quality of attention that sees things as they are without urgency. Not passive—urgency and engagement are different things. You can be fully engaged with something and feel no urgency about it. The urgency is what makes you start shaping the experience toward a conclusion before the experience is finished, filtering what you see through what you need the answer to be.

A doctor told me once that the hardest thing to teach medical students was how to sit with diagnostic uncertainty. Their instinct—a good instinct, in many contexts—was to move toward resolution, to gather the information, form the hypothesis, commit to it. But the best diagnosticians, she said, could hold multiple hypotheses in tension for longer than was comfortable. They didn't treat uncertainty as a problem to be solved as quickly as possible. They treated it as information, the uncertainty itself telling them something about what they were looking at.

I think this is related to the difference between asking "what is this?" and asking "what should I do with this?" The second question is often necessary, but it arrives too early. It colonizes the first question. And then you've stopped seeing what's there and started seeing what you've already decided to do with it.

---

I've been writing this without a destination in mind, which is either its strength or its problem, and possibly both. I started with maps because I happened to read something about the Waldseemüller map recently and it lodged in my thinking. I don't know exactly why. Usually when something lodges, it's because it's snagged on something else already there, something unresolved.

What's unresolved, I think, is a question about the relationship between knowledge and honesty. The medieval cartographers who drew monsters in the blank spaces were not wrong, exactly—or they were wrong in a particular direction, filling absence with imagination rather than leaving it as absence. The cartographers who left the spaces blank were right in a different way, but blank spaces are uncomfortable. They invite distortion. Someone else will fill them.

And then there's Waldseemüller, who had knowledge he had no official authority to have, and drew it anyway, and turned out to be more right than the official maps.

I think what I'm reaching toward is something like: the most honest engagement with the world involves being willing to sit with the blank spaces without filling them prematurely, but also being willing to trust what you know even when you can't account for how you know it. Those two things seem to be in tension. They require a kind of calibration that I don't think you ever fully achieve—you keep adjusting, misjudging, correcting.

My friend with the perfect memory never quite figured out how to let her past become sediment. I don't think that's her fault. I think she was just built a certain way, the way some people have nerve disorders that prevent them from feeling pain properly—that category error of the body where it treats something that should be integrated as perpetually raw.

The rest of us forget, and are metabolized by our own forgetting, and lose things that maybe we should have held, and are free of things we couldn't afford to keep carrying. We navigate by a map that keeps revising itself. The edges are uncertain. There are, in all likelihood, blank spaces we haven't reached yet, and some things we know that we'll never be able to account for, and somewhere on the margin of everything, whether or not we put them there, the dragons.

---

If I were being strictly honest about why I started thinking about maps today—it was not the Waldseemüller article alone. It was a conversation I had with someone I've known for a long time, in which I realized that my map of who that person was had not been updated in years. I'd been navigating by old information. I'd been confidently pointing to roads that had closed, features that had changed. I was, in the most literal metaphorical sense, lost.

And the feeling of realizing you're lost—really lost, not GPS-lost but lost in the older sense—is not entirely unpleasant. That's the thing they don't tell you. There is something that clears in you when the false confidence gives way. The world gets briefly more vivid. You have to actually look at what's in front of you instead of at the map.

You have to start from where you are.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
